title: Does CLIP’s generalization performance mainly stem from high train-test similarity?

reviews.summary:
This paper tries to uncover the reason for the good generalization of foundation models like CLIP. Specifically, we see that CLIP has a good zero-shot accuracy on OOD datasets, which is much higher compared to the models that are first trained on the labeled dataset (ID), such as ImageNet, and then tested on OOD datasets, such as ImageNet-Sketch.

As the pre-training data is large, it is highly likely to contain similar images to the OOD dataset. If that is the case, we might not call the test OOD dataset to be truly out-of-distribution, which might be the reason for the good performance of CLIP. 

Authors try to find an answer to the above problem through various experiments. 

They define the perceptual similarity of two images using the CLIP ViT-B/16 embedding of a pre-trained CLIP model. They find that the general OOD datasets like ImageNet Sketch have more common images with the LAION dataset than the ImageNet dataset.

In the first experiment, the authors removed images similar to the OOD dataset from the LAION dataset iteratively and measured the performance of models trained on a pruned dataset. As expected, they found a general trend of performance decrease, indicating that similar images in the training dataset had more importance.

The second experiment compared the models trained on small datasets such as ImageNet vs those trained on LAION. The authors define a metric called generalization gap, which is the set of minimum distance of each image in the test set with every image in the training set. They then remove the data points from the larger dataset (here, LAION) to have the same generalization gap as that of the ImageNet with the OOD dataset. They then trained a CLIP model on the pruned dataset. 
They discovered some performance drops, but the performance was still high compared to the case where the model was just trained on the ImageNet training set. This led to them concluding that the high performance of the CLIP is mostly due to more training data rather than test train similarity.
reviews.strengths: The paper is very well written. The experiments are clearly defined, and the motivation for each experiment is mentioned.
reviews.weaknesses:
Even though authors perform many experiments, the experiments are performed on a single type of dataset, which are the variations of the ImageNet.
It would have been to include experiments with other OOD datasets such as iWILDCam or FMoW. Without these experiments it is not sure if these analysis is limited to one type of dataset.
reviews.questions:
1. Are there experiments on other datasets unrelated to ImageNet? Having these experiments would be better to make a strong case.
2. Why do we use only CLIP ViT-B/16 embeddings to filter? Won't larger CLIP models have a better embedding to filter out the data points?
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

Summary:
This paper tries to uncover the reason for the good generalization of foundation models like CLIP. Specifically, we see that CLIP has a good zero-shot accuracy on OOD datasets, which is much higher compared to the models that are first trained on the labeled dataset (ID), such as ImageNet, and then tested on OOD datasets, such as ImageNet-Sketch.

As the pre-training data is large, it is highly likely to contain similar images to the OOD dataset. If that is the case, we might not call the test OOD dataset to be truly out-of-distribution, which might be the reason for the good performance of CLIP. 

Authors try to find an answer to the above problem through various experiments. 

They define the perceptual similarity of two images using the CLIP ViT-B/16 embedding of a pre-trained CLIP model. They find that the general OOD datasets like ImageNet Sketch have more common images with the LAION dataset than the ImageNet dataset.

In the first experiment, the authors removed images similar to the OOD dataset from the LAION dataset iteratively and measured the performance of models trained on a pruned dataset. As expected, they found a general trend of performance decrease, indicating that similar images in the training dataset had more importance.

The second experiment compared the models trained on small datasets such as ImageNet vs those trained on LAION. The authors define a metric called generalization gap, which is the set of minimum distance of each image in the test set with every image in the training set. They then remove the data points from the larger dataset (here, LAION) to have the same generalization gap as that of the ImageNet with the OOD dataset. They then trained a CLIP model on the pruned dataset. 
They discovered some performance drops, but the performance was still high compared to the case where the model was just trained on the ImageNet training set. This led to them concluding that the high performance of the CLIP is mostly due to more training data rather than test train similarity.

Strengths:
The paper is very well written. The experiments are clearly defined, and the motivation for each experiment is mentioned.

Weaknesses:
Even though authors perform many experiments, the experiments are performed on a single type of dataset, which are the variations of the ImageNet.
It would have been to include experiments with other OOD datasets such as iWILDCam or FMoW. Without these experiments it is not sure if these analysis is limited to one type of dataset.

Questions:
1. Are there experiments on other datasets unrelated to ImageNet? Having these experiments would be better to make a strong case.
2. Why do we use only CLIP ViT-B/16 embeddings to filter? Won't larger CLIP models have a better embedding to filter out the data points?

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Hybrid Peer Review  

### Summary  
This paper investigates whether CLIP’s strong generalization on out-of-distribution (OOD) benchmarks (e.g., ImageNet-Sketch) stems primarily from high train-test similarity—i.e., the presence of perceptually similar images in its web-scale training data (LAION). The authors formalize a perceptual similarity metric using CLIP ViT-B/16 embeddings and demonstrate that LAION contains more images similar to OOD benchmarks than ImageNet-Train. Through two key experiments—iterative pruning of LAION to remove near-OOD samples and alignment of LAION’s "similarity gap" (minimum test-train image similarity) to match ImageNet’s—they show that CLIP’s performance drops only marginally after pruning. This suggests high train-test similarity alone cannot explain CLIP’s robustness, implicating other data properties like scale and diversity. A secondary contribution is a curated 100M LAION subset that preserves CLIP’s performance.  

### Strengths  
1. **Clarity & Structure**: The paper is well-written, with clearly motivated experiments and intuitive visualizations (e.g., similarity distributions, nearest neighbors).  
2. **Novelty**: The formalization of "similarity gaps" and their use to prune LAION provides a rigorous method to test a prevalent assumption about foundation models.  
3. **Technical Soundness**: Experiments are thorough, with ablations across multiple benchmarks (ImageNet-Sketch/R/A) and controls for dataset size. The release of code and the 100M dataset enhances reproducibility.  
4. **Significance**: The findings meaningfully challenge the notion that CLIP’s OOD performance is primarily similarity-driven, highlighting the role of data scale/diversity.  

### Weaknesses  
1. **Limited Benchmark Scope**: Experiments focus exclusively on ImageNet-derived OOD datasets (e.g., Sketch, R, A). Broader evaluation on non-ImageNet benchmarks (e.g., iWILDCam, FMoW, medical/satellite imagery) is needed to confirm generalizability.  
2. **Reliance on CLIP for Similarity**: The perceptual similarity metric uses CLIP embeddings exclusively (ViT-B/16 and ViT-L/14), creating potential circularity. Ablations with non-CLIP metrics (e.g., DINO) or human judgments are absent.  
3. **Unexplored Data Properties**: While scale/diversity are implicated, specific factors (e.g., caption quality, semantic diversity) driving post-pruning performance remain unanalyzed.  
4. **Methodological Gaps**: The handling of ImageNet-Train captions during integration with LAION is ambiguous, and the impact of pruning order for multi-benchmark subsets ("combined-pruned") is unclear.  

### Questions  
1. **Benchmark Generality**: Would results hold for non-ImageNet OOD tasks (e.g., COCO, CelebA, or domain-specific datasets like iWILDCam)?  
2. **Similarity Metric Sensitivity**: How would alternative metrics (e.g., DINO embeddings or human similarity judgments) affect pruning outcomes and conclusions?  
3. **Compositional Generalization**: Could CLIP’s residual performance stem from composing features (e.g., "sketch" + "class label") without seeing similar examples?  
4. **Data Curation**: For the 100M subset, does semantic diversity align with full LAION? How does caption quality impact results when integrating ImageNet-Train?  
5. **Over-Pruning Concerns**: For the "combined-pruned" dataset, does the larger performance drop reflect over-pruning? How was diversity preserved across benchmarks?  

### Decision  
**Rating: 6 (marginally above the acceptance threshold)**  
While the paper’s core premise—challenging the similarity hypothesis via similarity-gap alignment—is original and methodologically sound, its narrow focus on ImageNet-derived benchmarks limits the scope of its claims. The reliance on CLIP-based similarity metrics and insufficient exploration of alternative data properties further constrain its impact. However, the rigorous pruning framework, valuable dataset release, and clear presentation justify marginal acceptance. Addressing the benchmark scope and metric limitations in future work would strengthen the contribution significantly.

reviews.summary: This paper studies the generalization behaviour of CLIP from the perspective of the training set. CLIP, as a pioneering foundation model, is well known for its exceptional generalization capability. However, it remains unclear whether such a good generalization capability stems from the web-scale training set, as it may well enclose samples similar to those on the test sets. This paper tackles this question by filtering out similar samples according to CLIP-embedding-based nearest neighbors. By creating a LAION subset that comes with as large a generalization gap as the ImageNet-1K training set, this work concludes that it is other factors, rather than the model has already seen samples with similar distribution, that leads to the outstanding ood generalization of CLIP models.
reviews.strengths:
1. This paper first studies the ood generalization behaviour of CLIP from the interesting perspective of train-test distribution similarity, and has drawn some intriguing conclusions, like a subset of 100M of LAION-400M is able to train a good-performing model on ood benchmarks.
2. The experiments are well motivated and designed, resulting in compelling results that even without nearest neighbor samples, CLIP is still to maintain good performance on ood benchmarks.
reviews.weaknesses:
1. This paper only works on common classification ood benchmarks, but neglecting a whole bunch of other CLIP application domains. In the very least, retrieval is the most foundamental test ground to probe how good a CLIP model is. The authors could use MSCOCO as the in-domain dataset for sample filtering, and use Flickr-30k as the out-of-domain dataset. Also, experiments on more diverse benchmarks, like VTAB (check datacomp paper for current best practices), are encouraged.
2. Some crucial experimental details seem to be missing. For instance, how the nearest neighbor sets in section 4.1 are constructed are not mentioned at all (or mentioned in the appendix). How many samples are chosen in this set, and what is the threshold of CLIP score used here?
3. The authors should compare the similarity of nearest neighbors to test sets between the whole LAION datset (after filtering in section 5/6) and ImageNet-Train. Otherwise, one could argue that, even though the closest sample in LAION and ImageNet training set is about the same far away to the test set, LAION has a lot more samples close to the closest sample than ImageNet (but not as close to the test set as the closest sample), and thus still has a unfair advantage on those ood benchmarks, compromising the validity of the conclusion.
reviews.questions: What is the potential application of the finding in this paper? I know this is a pure analytical work, and believe its merit is above the aceeptance threshold of ICLR. But I am still intrigued to learn what is the broader impact of this work, like how this would enlighten future research or engineer endeavours.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper studies the generalization behaviour of CLIP from the perspective of the training set. CLIP, as a pioneering foundation model, is well known for its exceptional generalization capability. However, it remains unclear whether such a good generalization capability stems from the web-scale training set, as it may well enclose samples similar to those on the test sets. This paper tackles this question by filtering out similar samples according to CLIP-embedding-based nearest neighbors. By creating a LAION subset that comes with as large a generalization gap as the ImageNet-1K training set, this work concludes that it is other factors, rather than the model has already seen samples with similar distribution, that leads to the outstanding ood generalization of CLIP models.

Strengths:
1. This paper first studies the ood generalization behaviour of CLIP from the interesting perspective of train-test distribution similarity, and has drawn some intriguing conclusions, like a subset of 100M of LAION-400M is able to train a good-performing model on ood benchmarks.
2. The experiments are well motivated and designed, resulting in compelling results that even without nearest neighbor samples, CLIP is still to maintain good performance on ood benchmarks.

Weaknesses:
1. This paper only works on common classification ood benchmarks, but neglecting a whole bunch of other CLIP application domains. In the very least, retrieval is the most foundamental test ground to probe how good a CLIP model is. The authors could use MSCOCO as the in-domain dataset for sample filtering, and use Flickr-30k as the out-of-domain dataset. Also, experiments on more diverse benchmarks, like VTAB (check datacomp paper for current best practices), are encouraged.
2. Some crucial experimental details seem to be missing. For instance, how the nearest neighbor sets in section 4.1 are constructed are not mentioned at all (or mentioned in the appendix). How many samples are chosen in this set, and what is the threshold of CLIP score used here?
3. The authors should compare the similarity of nearest neighbors to test sets between the whole LAION datset (after filtering in section 5/6) and ImageNet-Train. Otherwise, one could argue that, even though the closest sample in LAION and ImageNet training set is about the same far away to the test set, LAION has a lot more samples close to the closest sample than ImageNet (but not as close to the test set as the closest sample), and thus still has a unfair advantage on those ood benchmarks, compromising the validity of the conclusion.

Questions:
What is the potential application of the finding in this paper? I know this is a pure analytical work, and believe its merit is above the aceeptance threshold of ICLR. But I am still intrigued to learn what is the broader impact of this work, like how this would enlighten future research or engineer endeavours.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Hybrid Review

**Summary:**  
This paper investigates whether CLIP's exceptional out-of-distribution (OOD) generalization stems primarily from high similarity between its web-scale training data (LAION) and test sets, potentially enclosing samples stylistically or semantically similar to OOD benchmarks. The authors tackle this by introducing perceptual similarity metrics based on CLIP embeddings, formalizing the concept of a 'similarity gap,' and systematically pruning LAION to match ImageNet-Train’s similarity gap to various OOD benchmarks (e.g., ImageNet-Sketch/R/A). Surprisingly, CLIP models trained on these pruned subsets exhibit only marginal performance drops on most benchmarks (e.g., 10.8% relative drop on ImageNet-Sketch), indicating that factors beyond train-test similarity—such as dataset scale and diversity—drive generalization. As a secondary contribution, the authors curate a 100M LAION subset that maintains full performance when used for training.

**Strengths:**  
1.  **Original Perspective:** The paper innovatively challenges a common assumption about foundation models by testing the role of train-test similarity through rigorous similarity-gap alignment. The formalization of this gap is a key conceptual contribution.  
2.  **Compelling Methodology:** Experiments are well-motivated, thorough, and technically sound. The pruning strategy provides strong evidence that CLIP maintains robust performance even after removing highly similar samples. The release of the curated 100M subset enhances reproducibility and utility for future research.  
3.  **Significant Implications:** The findings meaningfully advance understanding of CLIP’s robustness, demonstrating that its generalization cannot be primarily attributed to data similarity. This has important implications for data-centric AI and foundation model research.  

**Weaknesses:**  
1.  **Limited Benchmark Scope:** While the OOD classification benchmarks (ImageNet-Sketch/R/A) are relevant, the study neglects other critical CLIP application domains. At minimum, image-text retrieval (e.g., using MSCOCO for in-domain filtering and Flickr-30k for OOD testing) should be included. Broader evaluation on diverse benchmarks like VTAB (following DataComp best practices) is also needed to strengthen generalizability claims.  
2.  **Methodological Concerns:**  
    -   **Missing Details:** Crucial experimental details are omitted, such as the construction of nearest-neighbor sets (Section 4.1), including the number of samples chosen and the CLIP score thresholds used.  
    -   **Similarity Metric Reliance:** The exclusive reliance on CLIP embeddings for similarity computation introduces potential circularity and bias. Ablations with alternative metrics (e.g., DINO, human judgments) or models are needed to confirm robustness. Appendix E’s brief comparison is insufficient.  
    -   **Incomplete Similarity Analysis:** The authors should directly compare the *distribution* of similarities between the entire LAION dataset (or pruned subsets) and ImageNet-Train relative to test sets. It remains possible that LAION retains many samples moderately closer to test sets than ImageNet, even after pruning the closest samples, potentially conferring an advantage.  
3.  **Unexplored Mechanisms:** While the paper attributes residual performance to scale/diversity, it doesn’t investigate specific properties (e.g., semantic diversity, caption quality) or mechanisms like compositional generalization (e.g., combining 'sketch' and 'dog' concepts without direct examples). Caption standardization when integrating ImageNet-Train with LAION also warrants scrutiny regarding its impact on text-image alignment.  

**Questions:**  
1.  **Scope & Generalizability:** Given the focus on ImageNet-derived benchmarks, would the core findings hold for non-ImageNet OOD tasks (e.g., medical imaging, remote sensing, or retrieval tasks like MSCOCO/Flickr-30k)?  
2.  **Similarity Metric Robustness:** How sensitive are the pruning outcomes and performance results to the choice of similarity metric? Did preliminary experiments with non-CLIP models (e.g., DINO) or human judgments yield consistent conclusions, or could they alter the interpretation?  
3.  **Data Properties & Applications:** Beyond scale, what specific properties of the pruned datasets (especially the 100M subset) drive the maintained performance? How could this insight enlighten future dataset curation or model training endeavors? What is the broader impact for understanding foundation models?  
4.  **Combined Pruning & Dataset Quality:** For the 'combined-pruned' dataset, how were conflicting similarity gaps across benchmarks resolved? Could the larger performance drop indicate over-pruning, and how was semantic diversity preserved during multi-benchmark pruning? How does the 100M core set compare to LAION's full diversity for non-ImageNet tasks?  

**Decision:**  
This paper presents a novel and methodologically rigorous investigation into the drivers of CLIP's OOD generalization, effectively challenging the prevalent similarity hypothesis through well-designed pruning experiments. The core finding—that CLIP maintains strong performance even when trained on data exhibiting ImageNet-level similarity gaps to test sets—is significant and well-supported. The release of the 100M subset is a valuable contribution. However, the work is limited by its narrow benchmark scope (lacking retrieval and broader OOD tasks), insufficient exploration of alternative similarity metrics and underlying data properties, and missing methodological details. These limitations prevent the work from fully realizing its potential impact. While the core insight is compelling and merits attention, the noted weaknesses hold it back from a higher rating.

**Rating:**  
**6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold**

Summary:

6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: The goal of this work is to understand whether the superior OOD performance of foundation models like CLIP is a result of the training dataset containing images that are very similar to the OOD test set. Towards this, the authors systematically create several splits of the base LAION dataset that was used for training OpenCLIP. Firstly, they find that pruning samples that are very similar to the OOD test sets results in a considerable drop in the OOD performance. However, by matching the train-test similarity with that of ImageNet for a fair comparison, the authors find that CLIP still shows significant gains when compared to an ImageNet pretrained model. Thus, although LAION contains images that are very similar to ImageNet-OOD test sets, this is not the key reason for better OOD generalization of CLIP. Understanding the reasons for better generalization of CLIP still remains an open question.
reviews.strengths:
- The key finding that despite reducing the similarity between the training data and the test sets, there is an improvement in test set performance, is helpful. 
- Several insightful experimental results are presented, which is helpful for the community, especially given that these experiments are very computationally intensive. 
- The pruned datasets whose code is released, can help with further investigation on why CLIP models have better OOD performance.
reviews.weaknesses:
- Although the results are interesting, my main concern is that the analysis is not sufficient to enable fair **OOD** testing. If the paper was about **ID** performance alone, removing train set samples that are similar to each test sample would have been sufficient, as reported in the paper. However, **OOD** implies that the **distribution** of images is unknown. So, to actually conclude that OOD evaluation is fair, all images from the test **domain** should have been removed, not only the images that are similar to every test set image. Therefore, as mentioned in the abstract, the term "out-of-distribution generalization" is still not meaningful even by training on the pruned datasets considered in the paper.
- To elaborate, if there are 5 sketches of the class "airplane" in the dataset, all images that are close to these sketches are removed. But there may be other airplane sketches, which are farther away than the closest image in ImageNet-test set, which are not removed. Thus, it is not guaranteed that all images of the given classes and test **domain** are removed from the train set.
- Further, there may be other objects, such as "space shuttle"  which are not included in ImageNet, thus sketches of space shuttles can be present in the training data, in addition to natural images of the same. Thus the domain "sketch" is not unseen by CLIP, and hence the evaluation is not truly OOD. 

Minor feedback: 
- Clarity of the abstract and contributions list needs improvement. It would be better to make a shorter summary of the key contributions.
- It would be better to give a different name to "generalization gap" as this term is used in a different context.

Typo:

"We provide anecdotal evidence in Fig. 1 where we choose samples from ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-R and examine their nearest perceptual neighbors in LAION-400M and **ImageNet**"
reviews.questions:
Several additional experiments are required to be done, in order to emulate a true **OOD** setting, as discussed in the weaknesses section.
For example, one should remove all "sketch" style images as well, from L-200M+IN-Train (sketch-pruned) in order to test on the ImageNet-sketch test set for a true OOD evaluation. Otherwise, other sketch images that are present in the dataset can help bridge the domain gap between sketches and natural images, leading to an unfair OOD evaluation.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The goal of this work is to understand whether the superior OOD performance of foundation models like CLIP is a result of the training dataset containing images that are very similar to the OOD test set. Towards this, the authors systematically create several splits of the base LAION dataset that was used for training OpenCLIP. Firstly, they find that pruning samples that are very similar to the OOD test sets results in a considerable drop in the OOD performance. However, by matching the train-test similarity with that of ImageNet for a fair comparison, the authors find that CLIP still shows significant gains when compared to an ImageNet pretrained model. Thus, although LAION contains images that are very similar to ImageNet-OOD test sets, this is not the key reason for better OOD generalization of CLIP. Understanding the reasons for better generalization of CLIP still remains an open question.

Strengths:
- The key finding that despite reducing the similarity between the training data and the test sets, there is an improvement in test set performance, is helpful. 
- Several insightful experimental results are presented, which is helpful for the community, especially given that these experiments are very computationally intensive. 
- The pruned datasets whose code is released, can help with further investigation on why CLIP models have better OOD performance.

Weaknesses:
- Although the results are interesting, my main concern is that the analysis is not sufficient to enable fair **OOD** testing. If the paper was about **ID** performance alone, removing train set samples that are similar to each test sample would have been sufficient, as reported in the paper. However, **OOD** implies that the **distribution** of images is unknown. So, to actually conclude that OOD evaluation is fair, all images from the test **domain** should have been removed, not only the images that are similar to every test set image. Therefore, as mentioned in the abstract, the term "out-of-distribution generalization" is still not meaningful even by training on the pruned datasets considered in the paper.
- To elaborate, if there are 5 sketches of the class "airplane" in the dataset, all images that are close to these sketches are removed. But there may be other airplane sketches, which are farther away than the closest image in ImageNet-test set, which are not removed. Thus, it is not guaranteed that all images of the given classes and test **domain** are removed from the train set.
- Further, there may be other objects, such as "space shuttle"  which are not included in ImageNet, thus sketches of space shuttles can be present in the training data, in addition to natural images of the same. Thus the domain "sketch" is not unseen by CLIP, and hence the evaluation is not truly OOD. 

Minor feedback: 
- Clarity of the abstract and contributions list needs improvement. It would be better to make a shorter summary of the key contributions.
- It would be better to give a different name to "generalization gap" as this term is used in a different context.

Typo:

"We provide anecdotal evidence in Fig. 1 where we choose samples from ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-R and examine their nearest perceptual neighbors in LAION-400M and **ImageNet**"

Questions:
Several additional experiments are required to be done, in order to emulate a true **OOD** setting, as discussed in the weaknesses section.
For example, one should remove all "sketch" style images as well, from L-200M+IN-Train (sketch-pruned) in order to test on the ImageNet-sketch test set for a true OOD evaluation. Otherwise, other sketch images that are present in the dataset can help bridge the domain gap between sketches and natural images, leading to an unfair OOD evaluation.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Hybrid Review  

**Summary:**  
This work investigates whether CLIP's superior out-of-distribution (OOD) performance stems primarily from high train-test similarity in web-scale datasets like LAION. The authors introduce perceptual similarity metrics (using CLIP embeddings), formalize the "similarity gap" concept, and systematically prune LAION to match ImageNet's similarity gap to OOD benchmarks (e.g., ImageNet-Sketch/R/A). While pruning near-duplicates causes a performance drop, retraining CLIP on these pruned datasets yields only marginal reductions (e.g., 10.8% on ImageNet-Sketch)—far exceeding ImageNet-trained models. This indicates that high similarity alone cannot explain CLIP’s OOD generalization, implicating scale/diversity as key factors. The authors also curate a 100M LAION subset that maintains full performance, released for reproducibility.  

**Strengths:**  
- **Novel Experimental Design:** The similarity-gap alignment framework provides a rigorous, original methodology to isolate the impact of train-test similarity. This effectively challenges prevalent assumptions about foundation models.  
- **Valuable Insights & Resources:** Results demonstrate that CLIP’s robustness persists even after aggressive pruning, highlighting the role of data properties beyond similarity. The released pruned datasets and code significantly aid community research.  
- **Technical Rigor:** Experiments are thorough, computationally intensive, and span multiple benchmarks. Controls (e.g., integrating ImageNet-Train with standardized captions) bolster validity. The 100M core subset is a practical contribution.  

**Weaknesses:**  
- **Fundamental Flaw in OOD Evaluation:** The pruning strategy does not guarantee a truly *unseen domain* for OOD testing. Removing samples similar to *specific test images* (e.g., sketches of ImageNet classes) does not eliminate the entire domain (e.g., *all* sketches). Non-ImageNet objects (e.g., "space shuttle" sketches) or stylistically similar images may remain, enabling domain-specific feature learning. Thus, claims about "OOD generalization" remain problematic.  
- **Narrow Benchmark Scope:** Evaluation is restricted to ImageNet-derived OOD sets (e.g., Sketch/R/A). Findings may not generalize to truly distinct domains like medical or satellite imagery.  
- **Methodological Biases:** Reliance on CLIP embeddings for similarity computation risks circularity. Alternative metrics (e.g., DINO, human perception) are underexplored (Appendix E is brief), potentially skewing pruning outcomes.  
- **Limited Investigation of Data Properties:** While scale/diversity are implicated, the work does not identify *specific* attributes (e.g., caption quality, semantic coverage) driving residual performance.  

**Minor Feedback:**  
- **Clarity:** The abstract and contributions should be condensed for sharper focus. "Generalization gap" is ambiguous due to conflicting uses in literature; consider renaming (e.g., "train-test similarity gap").  
- **Figure Consistency:** Resolve minor formatting issues (e.g., incomplete captions in Fig 1/2).  
- **Terminology:** Replace references to "anecdotal evidence" (Fig. 1) with "qualitative analysis."  

**Questions:**  
1. Can you validate your pruning strategy for *domain-level* OOD fairness? For example, does removing *all* sketch-style images from L-200M+IN-Train (not just those similar to ImageNet-Sketch) further degrade performance on Sketch tests?  
2. How sensitive are results to the similarity metric? Would pruning with non-CLIP models (e.g., DINO) or human judgments alter conclusions?  
3. Does CLIP’s resilience stem from compositional generalization (e.g., combining "sketch" + "object" concepts without explicit pairs)? Could this be tested?  
4. Why restrict evaluation to ImageNet-based benchmarks? Would similarity-gap pruning yield similar results for non-ImageNet OOD tasks (e.g., CelebA)?  
5. For the combined-pruned dataset, how were conflicting similarity gaps across benchmarks resolved? Could over-pruning explain its larger performance drop?  

**Decision:**  
**Rating: 6 — Marginally above the acceptance threshold**  
This paper makes a valuable empirical contribution by debunking the simplicity of attributing CLIP’s OOD gains to train-test similarity. The methodology is innovative, the experiments are resource-intensive, and the curated datasets are useful. However, the core OOD evaluation remains methodologically unsound due to domain contamination, and key limitations (narrow benchmarks, CLIP-centric metrics) weaken broader claims. While the findings advance the discourse on foundation models, revisions addressing these caveats are needed for conclusive OOD insights.  

---  
**Key Integrations:**  
- **Strengths:** Incorporated ideas from alternative reviews praising novelty, rigor, and dataset utility.  
- **Weaknesses:** Retained the main review’s core critique on OOD validity while integrating concerns about benchmark scope (Alt 3,4), metric bias (Alt 1,2,3,4), and unexplored data properties (Alt 1,2).  
- **Questions:** Combined the main review’s domain-pruning experiment with alternative questions on metrics (Alt 1,2,3), compositionality (Alt 1,2), and benchmark scope (Alt 2,3,4).  
- **Tone/Structure:** Maintained the main review’s critical stance but adopted the alternatives’ structured framing (e.g., "methodological biases").  
- **Decision:** Unchanged from the main review (6) despite the alternatives’ higher ratings, as weaknesses in OOD validity remain unresolved.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
The goal of this work is to understand whether the superior OOD performance of foundation models like CLIP is a result of the training dataset containing images that are very similar to the OOD test set. Towards this, the authors systematically create several splits of the base LAION dataset that was used for training OpenCLIP. Firstly, they find that pruning samples that are very similar to the OOD test sets results in a considerable drop in the OOD performance. However, by matching the train-test similarity with that of ImageNet for a fair comparison, the authors find that CLIP still shows significant gains when compared to an ImageNet pretrained model. Thus, although LAION contains images that are very similar to ImageNet-OOD test sets, this is not the key reason for better OOD generalization of CLIP. Understanding the reasons for better generalization of CLIP still remains an open question.

Strengths:
- The key finding that despite reducing the similarity between the training data and the test sets, there is an improvement in test set performance, is helpful. 
- Several insightful experimental results are presented, which is helpful for the community, especially given that these experiments are very computationally intensive. 
- The pruned datasets whose code is released, can help with further investigation on why CLIP models have better OOD performance.

Weaknesses:
- Although the results are interesting, my main concern is that the analysis is not sufficient to enable fair **OOD** testing. If the paper was about **ID** performance alone, removing train set samples that are similar to each test sample would have been sufficient, as reported in the paper. However, **OOD** implies that the **distribution** of images is unknown. So, to actually conclude that OOD evaluation is fair, all images from the test **domain** should have been removed, not only the images that are similar to every test set image. Therefore, as mentioned in the abstract, the term "out-of-distribution generalization" is still not meaningful even by training on the pruned datasets considered in the paper.
- To elaborate, if there are 5 sketches of the class "airplane" in the dataset, all images that are close to these sketches are removed. But there may be other airplane sketches, which are farther away than the closest image in ImageNet-test set, which are not removed. Thus, it is not guaranteed that all images of the given classes and test **domain** are removed from the train set.
- Further, there may be other objects, such as "space shuttle"  which are not included in ImageNet, thus sketches of space shuttles can be present in the training data, in addition to natural images of the same. Thus the domain "sketch" is not unseen by CLIP, and hence the evaluation is not truly OOD. 

Minor feedback: 
- Clarity of the abstract and contributions list needs improvement. It would be better to make a shorter summary of the key contributions.
- It would be better to give a different name to "generalization gap" as this term is used in a different context.

Typo:

"We provide anecdotal evidence in Fig. 1 where we choose samples from ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-R and examine their nearest perceptual neighbors in LAION-400M and **ImageNet**"

Questions:
Several additional experiments are required to be done, in order to emulate a true **OOD** setting, as discussed in the weaknesses section.
For example, one should remove all "sketch" style images as well, from L-200M+IN-Train (sketch-pruned) in order to test on the ImageNet-sketch test set for a true OOD evaluation. Otherwise, other sketch images that are present in the dataset can help bridge the domain gap between sketches and natural images, leading to an unfair OOD evaluation.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: This paper studies the hypothesis “Does CLIP’s accuracy on test sets mainly stem from highly similar images in its train set?”. Their approach is to take a CLIP training dataset, LAION, and remove samples similar to OOD benchmarks, then retrain and evaluate the performance drop. In Section 4.1, they show that for some OOD datasets e.g., ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-R, there are more similar images to the OOD dataset in LAION than there are in the ImageNet-train set. These images are both semantically and stylistically more similar. They also observe a positive correlation between accuracy and having similar neighbors in the training set for each OOD benchmark. In the remainder of the paper, they prune the dataset by removing similar samples to OOD datasets and evaluate the performance of models trained on pruned datasets.
reviews.strengths:
- Figure 1 clearly shows that the LAION-400M dataset contains semantically and stylistically similar images to OOD benchmarks while the ImageNet training set does not.
- Figure 3 quantitatively shows the nearest neighbors of OOD datasets in LAION-400M are on average more similar than the nearest neighbors from ImageNet-train set except for the Imagenet-Val set itself. Fig 3.right also clearly shows that CLIP model performs better on samples that it has seen similar images of it in the training set.
- Figure 4 shows that pruning LAION for similar samples to ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-Val results in substantial accuracy drop compared with pruning random samples. Hence showing that these similar samples are crucial for the effective robustness of CLIP models.
reviews.weaknesses:
- Even though the results in Figure 4 show the near-pruned samples are crucial for OOD generalization of CLIP, they are not conclusive. In particular, the following two experiments would be useful:
i) Does a model *only* lose OOD generalization on one benchmark or are these near-pruned samples crucial for all sorts of image-classification performance on any benchmark? This requires plotting the accuracy on two datasets, e.g., ImageNet-Val and ImageNet-Sketch, and showing that after pruning samples similar to ImageNet-Sketch, we only lose performance on ImageNet-Sketch.
ii) Do CLIP models lose OOD generalization more quickly than classification models trained on ImageNet? For this, one would prune ImageNet with a similar procedure for X% of samples and compare whether the accuracy drop in ImageNet models is slower than CLIP models. If not, then we would know that any OOD generalization we have been seeing could be due to seeing similar samples in the training set.

- This paper is a good place to have a broader discussion on “What is zero-shot?” and “What is out-of-distribution robustness?” and I think the paper should expand on that. In particular, after showing that pruning loses OOD generalization, it is not clear what the community should do with these datasets and evaluation benchmarks. Should we say CLIP models have cheated and they are not zero-shot? Related to that, the objective of Sections 5 and 6 that “Correct for highly similar images” is not clear. Why would we want to prune our training datasets to get worse on some test benchmarks if those exact test samples do not appear in the training set? Why would we want models in Table 1 trained on pruned datasets that perform worse?
reviews.questions:
- Figure 4: What if we perform a similar process for training on the ImageNet dataset? Do we observe a similar accuracy drop?
- Figure 7 shows that removing samples similar to ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-Val result in lower performance in all other ImageNet OOD datasets as well. Could this mean that these samples are important for a general understanding of the ImageNet distribution?
- Section 5: Would this method and any model trained on this dataset be considered as transductive learning? Because the test datasets would have been seen directly or indirectly by the model. Would this be against the license of datasets such as ObjectNet that say “ObjectNet may never be used to tune the parameters of any model.”?

Typos:
- Page 7: Let us for consider -> Let us consider

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper studies the hypothesis “Does CLIP’s accuracy on test sets mainly stem from highly similar images in its train set?”. Their approach is to take a CLIP training dataset, LAION, and remove samples similar to OOD benchmarks, then retrain and evaluate the performance drop. In Section 4.1, they show that for some OOD datasets e.g., ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-R, there are more similar images to the OOD dataset in LAION than there are in the ImageNet-train set. These images are both semantically and stylistically more similar. They also observe a positive correlation between accuracy and having similar neighbors in the training set for each OOD benchmark. In the remainder of the paper, they prune the dataset by removing similar samples to OOD datasets and evaluate the performance of models trained on pruned datasets.

Strengths:
- Figure 1 clearly shows that the LAION-400M dataset contains semantically and stylistically similar images to OOD benchmarks while the ImageNet training set does not.
- Figure 3 quantitatively shows the nearest neighbors of OOD datasets in LAION-400M are on average more similar than the nearest neighbors from ImageNet-train set except for the Imagenet-Val set itself. Fig 3.right also clearly shows that CLIP model performs better on samples that it has seen similar images of it in the training set.
- Figure 4 shows that pruning LAION for similar samples to ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-Val results in substantial accuracy drop compared with pruning random samples. Hence showing that these similar samples are crucial for the effective robustness of CLIP models.

Weaknesses:
- Even though the results in Figure 4 show the near-pruned samples are crucial for OOD generalization of CLIP, they are not conclusive. In particular, the following two experiments would be useful:
i) Does a model *only* lose OOD generalization on one benchmark or are these near-pruned samples crucial for all sorts of image-classification performance on any benchmark? This requires plotting the accuracy on two datasets, e.g., ImageNet-Val and ImageNet-Sketch, and showing that after pruning samples similar to ImageNet-Sketch, we only lose performance on ImageNet-Sketch.
ii) Do CLIP models lose OOD generalization more quickly than classification models trained on ImageNet? For this, one would prune ImageNet with a similar procedure for X% of samples and compare whether the accuracy drop in ImageNet models is slower than CLIP models. If not, then we would know that any OOD generalization we have been seeing could be due to seeing similar samples in the training set.

- This paper is a good place to have a broader discussion on “What is zero-shot?” and “What is out-of-distribution robustness?” and I think the paper should expand on that. In particular, after showing that pruning loses OOD generalization, it is not clear what the community should do with these datasets and evaluation benchmarks. Should we say CLIP models have cheated and they are not zero-shot? Related to that, the objective of Sections 5 and 6 that “Correct for highly similar images” is not clear. Why would we want to prune our training datasets to get worse on some test benchmarks if those exact test samples do not appear in the training set? Why would we want models in Table 1 trained on pruned datasets that perform worse?

Questions:
- Figure 4: What if we perform a similar process for training on the ImageNet dataset? Do we observe a similar accuracy drop?
- Figure 7 shows that removing samples similar to ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-Val result in lower performance in all other ImageNet OOD datasets as well. Could this mean that these samples are important for a general understanding of the ImageNet distribution?
- Section 5: Would this method and any model trained on this dataset be considered as transductive learning? Because the test datasets would have been seen directly or indirectly by the model. Would this be against the license of datasets such as ObjectNet that say “ObjectNet may never be used to tune the parameters of any model.”?

Typos:
- Page 7: Let us for consider -> Let us consider

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Hybrid Peer Review  

**Summary:**  
This paper investigates the hypothesis that CLIP’s accuracy on out-of-distribution (OOD) benchmarks primarily stems from highly similar images in its training data (LAION). The authors introduce perceptual similarity metrics using CLIP embeddings, demonstrate a correlation between similarity and performance, and formalize the concept of "similarity gaps." By pruning LAION to align with ImageNet’s similarity gap to OOD datasets (e.g., ImageNet-Sketch, ImageNet-R), they retrain models and observe only marginal performance drops (e.g., 10.8% on Sketch). This suggests high train-test similarity is insufficient to explain CLIP’s generalization. The work also releases a curated 100M LAION subset that maintains full performance.  

**Strengths:**  
- **Novel Methodology:** The formalization of "similarity gaps" and the pruning strategy to isolate factors driving generalization is a key conceptual contribution (Alt 1, Alt 2).  
- **Clear Visual Evidence:** Figure 1 effectively contrasts LAION’s semantically/stylistically similar images with ImageNet’s lack thereof. Figure 3 quantifies higher similarity of LAION neighbors to OOD benchmarks, while Figure 3 (right) shows CLIP excels on samples with similar training neighbors.  
- **Rigor:** Experiments are well-designed across multiple benchmarks (ImageNet-Sketch/R/A), with thorough ablations and controls (Alt 1, Alt 3). The 100M dataset release enhances reproducibility and utility for future research (Alt 1, Alt 4).  
- **Counterintuitive Insight:** Figure 4 shows pruning similar samples causes substantial accuracy drops compared to random pruning, yet post-pruning performance remains robust, implicating scale/diversity beyond similarity (Alt 2, Alt 3).  

**Weaknesses:**  
- **Inconclusive Causal Claims:** While Figure 4 shows pruning harms OOD robustness, the experiments lack two critical controls:  
  i) Testing whether performance drops are *benchmark-specific* (e.g., pruning Sketch samples should only harm Sketch, not ImageNet-Val).  
  ii) Comparing CLIP’s sensitivity to pruning against ImageNet-trained models. If both degrade similarly, this weakens claims about CLIP’s unique OOD reliance on similarity (Main Review).  
- **Unexplored Broader Implications:** The paper misses an opportunity to discuss foundational questions: What defines "zero-shot" or "OOD robustness"? If pruned datasets yield worse models (Table 1), why pursue them? Should benchmarks be redesigned if similarity gaps are inevitable at scale? (Main Review, Alt 3).  
- **Methodological Limitations:** Heavy reliance on CLIP embeddings for similarity computation risks circularity. While Appendix E notes CLIP embedding correlations, ablations with non-CLIP models (e.g., DINO) or human perception are absent (Alt 2, Alt 3).  
- **Narrow Benchmark Scope:** Evaluation is limited to ImageNet-derived OOD datasets. Findings may not generalize to non-ImageNet domains (e.g., medical/remote sensing) or tasks like object detection (Alt 3, Alt 4).  

**Questions:**  
1. **Pruning Sensitivity:** Does pruning ImageNet-Train using your method cause comparable accuracy drops? If so, does this imply all models (not just CLIP) rely on similarity for OOD robustness? (Main Review).  
2. **Similarity Metric Dependence:** How would results change with non-CLIP similarity metrics (e.g., DINO)? Could metric choice bias pruning outcomes? (Alt 1, Alt 3).  
3. **Compositional Generalization:** Could residual performance after pruning stem from CLIP composing features (e.g., "sketch" + "class") without seeing similar pairs? (Alt 1, Alt 2).  
4. **Transductive Learning Concerns:** Does pruning using test-set similarity (Section 5) constitute transductive learning? Does this violate licenses like ObjectNet’s prohibition on test-set tuning? (Main Review).  
5. **Core Set Generalizability:** Does the 100M subset maintain performance on non-ImageNet tasks? How does its diversity compare to LAION or deduplicated sets? (Alt 3, Alt 4).  

**Minor Issues:**  
- Page 7: Typo: "Let us for consider" → "Let us consider" (Main Review).  
- Figure 1/2 captions are incomplete in the main text (Alt 3).  

**Decision:**  
**Rating: 5 (Marginally below acceptance threshold)**  
While the paper addresses a timely question with methodological rigor and offers valuable resources (e.g., 100M dataset), critical weaknesses remain unresolved. The core experiments do not conclusively isolate the impact of similarity gaps on CLIP’s OOD robustness, and broader implications for benchmark design and "zero-shot" evaluation are underdeveloped. Methodological concerns about circularity in similarity metrics and narrow benchmark scope further limit the work’s impact. Addressing the key experiments (i-ii under Weaknesses) and engaging deeply with the conceptual questions raised would significantly strengthen the contribution.

title: Simple Hierarchical Planning with Diffusion

reviews.summary: The paper proposes Hierarchical Diffuser (HD), a sub-goal based method for planning with diffusion models. In recent literature, Diffuser [1] was proposed to plan an optimal sequence of state and action between initial state and goal. Following diffuser, the authors design two diffusers. One diffuser plans for sub-goals between the start and the goal state. For each sub-goal segment, the second diffuser plans the optimal state-action sequence. Experiments are conducted on Maze, AntMaze, MuJoCo Gym and FrankKitchen benchmarks. The performance of HD is compared with relevant offline RL and hierarchical RL algorithms. Further, the experiments show improved receptibility, OOD generalization and less computation overhead as compared to diffuser. Relevant ablations are performed.
reviews.strengths:
Discretizing planning into sub-goals with diffusion models is shown to be advantageous as more diverse scenarios can be solved. Low-level diffusion planning becomes task-agnostic.

Even the sparse diffuser or SD version is better than diffuser because of increased receptive field. The authors validate this by showing that increasing the kernel-size (hence the receptive field) of diffuser leads to better performance but weaker generalization.

SD with dense actions leads to better fitting of the sparse objective, a typical bottleneck in hierarchical RL.

HD takes lesser time than Diffuser due to shorter high-level sequence and parallelly solving low-level plans for all the segments.
reviews.weaknesses:
The formulation has limited novelty. While a single diffuser is not sufficient for planning over long-horizons, the work introduces two diffusers: one sparse diffuser for planning sequence of sub-goals between initial state and goal, while a standard diffuser solves for individual sub-goal segments.

Given that there are also methods which perform state-only diffusion like Decision-diffuser [2], is it possible to perform relevant ablations to justify why states are not sufficient for having a good estimate of $J$? If you are following diffuser’s codebase, are you using the diffusion sampled actions or a separate controller for Maze2d tasks like diffuser?

Solving for sub-goals independently without the knowledge about the final goal might lead to non-optimal behavior. How do you ensure that the sampled path for one segment does not overlap with the paths for the other segment? How many trajectories do you sample for individual segments and for the sub-goal sequence?

Also, what happens when actions do not lead to the exact sub-goal (unless you use inverse dynamics actions) for the maze case? Because there is no-feedback from the low-level diffuser to high-level diffuser, it might become challenging.

Overall, I acknowledge that the presented method is a promising revision of Diffuser which performs better in all aspects. However, I believe that the contributions are not significant enough.
reviews.questions: See weakness above.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The paper proposes Hierarchical Diffuser (HD), a sub-goal based method for planning with diffusion models. In recent literature, Diffuser [1] was proposed to plan an optimal sequence of state and action between initial state and goal. Following diffuser, the authors design two diffusers. One diffuser plans for sub-goals between the start and the goal state. For each sub-goal segment, the second diffuser plans the optimal state-action sequence. Experiments are conducted on Maze, AntMaze, MuJoCo Gym and FrankKitchen benchmarks. The performance of HD is compared with relevant offline RL and hierarchical RL algorithms. Further, the experiments show improved receptibility, OOD generalization and less computation overhead as compared to diffuser. Relevant ablations are performed.

Strengths:
Discretizing planning into sub-goals with diffusion models is shown to be advantageous as more diverse scenarios can be solved. Low-level diffusion planning becomes task-agnostic.

Even the sparse diffuser or SD version is better than diffuser because of increased receptive field. The authors validate this by showing that increasing the kernel-size (hence the receptive field) of diffuser leads to better performance but weaker generalization.

SD with dense actions leads to better fitting of the sparse objective, a typical bottleneck in hierarchical RL.

HD takes lesser time than Diffuser due to shorter high-level sequence and parallelly solving low-level plans for all the segments.

Weaknesses:
The formulation has limited novelty. While a single diffuser is not sufficient for planning over long-horizons, the work introduces two diffusers: one sparse diffuser for planning sequence of sub-goals between initial state and goal, while a standard diffuser solves for individual sub-goal segments.

Given that there are also methods which perform state-only diffusion like Decision-diffuser [2], is it possible to perform relevant ablations to justify why states are not sufficient for having a good estimate of $J$? If you are following diffuser’s codebase, are you using the diffusion sampled actions or a separate controller for Maze2d tasks like diffuser?

Solving for sub-goals independently without the knowledge about the final goal might lead to non-optimal behavior. How do you ensure that the sampled path for one segment does not overlap with the paths for the other segment? How many trajectories do you sample for individual segments and for the sub-goal sequence?

Also, what happens when actions do not lead to the exact sub-goal (unless you use inverse dynamics actions) for the maze case? Because there is no-feedback from the low-level diffuser to high-level diffuser, it might become challenging.

Overall, I acknowledge that the presented method is a promising revision of Diffuser which performs better in all aspects. However, I believe that the contributions are not significant enough.

Questions:
See weakness above.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Hybrid Review  

**Summary:**  
The paper proposes Hierarchical Diffuser (HD), a sub-goal based method for planning with diffusion models. Building on Diffuser [1], the authors design a two-level framework: a high-level "sparse diffuser" plans sub-goals between initial and goal states at fixed intervals (K steps), while low-level diffusers generate dense state-action sequences for each segment. Experiments across Maze2D, AntMaze, MuJoCo, and FrankaKitchen benchmarks demonstrate performance gains over baselines (e.g., +12% on Maze2D, +9.2% on MuJoCo vs. Diffuser), alongside 10× faster planning, improved generalization on compositional out-of-distribution (OOD) tasks, and reduced computational overhead. Ablations validate design choices, including kernel-size trade-offs and dense-action variants.  

**Strengths:**  
1. **Hierarchical Efficiency:** Discretizing planning into sub-goals enables task-agnostic low-level diffusion and significantly reduces computation. HD parallelizes low-level planning across segments, achieving 10× speedups in long-horizon tasks compared to Diffuser (Table 6).  
2. **Enhanced Generalization:** The hierarchical structure improves trajectory coverage and receptive field, validated through OOD tasks (Table 5) and kernel-size ablations. The sparse diffuser (SD) variant outperforms Diffuser even without dense actions, highlighting architectural benefits.  
3. **Robust Empirical Validation:** Extensive benchmarks against offline/hierarchical RL baselines (e.g., HDMI) show consistent gains. The dense-action variant (HD-DA) mitigates hierarchical RL bottlenecks by improving guidance function learning.  
4. **Theoretical Grounding:** Analysis of generalization-performance trade-offs (Theorem 1) provides insight into HD’s OOD robustness, though empirical validation remains limited.  

**Weaknesses:**  
1. **Limited Novelty:** While effective, HD repurposes Diffuser for sub-goal generation via fixed-interval subsampling—a simple extension. The dual-diffuser design lacks significant innovation beyond hierarchical partitioning. Comparisons to skill-based methods (e.g., latent skill planning) are absent, weakening contextualization.  
2. **Sub-goal Rigidity:** Fixed intervals (K) may not adapt to variable temporal abstractions, potentially limiting complex task performance. The authors acknowledge this but omit exploration of adaptive K or uncertainty-aware mechanisms.  
3. **Implementation Risks:** Solving segments independently risks suboptimal overlaps or compounding errors if actions miss sub-goals (e.g., Maze2D). The lack of feedback between low/high-level diffusers exacerbates this. Sampling strategies (e.g., trajectory counts per segment) are unclear.  
4. **Dataset Dependency:** Performance relies heavily on dataset quality. Ablations justifying state-only diffusion (vs. Decision Diffuser [2]) or action-sampling methods (e.g., inverse dynamics) are insufficient.  

**Questions:**  
1. How does fixed K handle environments with non-uniform temporal abstractions? Could adaptive K (e.g., via uncertainty estimation) improve results?  
2. How many trajectories are sampled per segment? How do you mitigate path overlaps or compounding errors when actions deviate from sub-goals?  
3. For Maze2D, are diffusion-sampled actions used directly, or is a separate controller employed (as in Diffuser)?  
4. Why not compare to skill-based hierarchical RL (e.g., reusable primitives), and can Theorem 1’s generalization bounds be empirically validated?  
5. Does padding sub-trajectories to fixed lengths impact variable-horizon tasks? Are alternatives (e.g., latent embeddings) feasible for reducing HD-DA’s computational overhead?  

**Decision:**  
The paper presents a well-executed extension of Diffuser that achieves tangible efficiency and generalization improvements. While the empirical validation is thorough—spanning 12+ benchmarks, ablations, and OOD tests—the core hierarchical mechanism lacks novelty, and limitations (fixed sub-goals, dataset sensitivity) constrain broader impact. The work is a **solid incremental contribution** but falls short of transformative advancement. I recommend **acceptance as a marginal candidate** (Rating: 6).
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
The paper proposes Hierarchical Diffuser (HD), a sub-goal based method for planning with diffusion models. In recent literature, Diffuser [1] was proposed to plan an optimal sequence of state and action between initial state and goal. Following diffuser, the authors design two diffusers. One diffuser plans for sub-goals between the start and the goal state. For each sub-goal segment, the second diffuser plans the optimal state-action sequence. Experiments are conducted on Maze, AntMaze, MuJoCo Gym and FrankKitchen benchmarks. The performance of HD is compared with relevant offline RL and hierarchical RL algorithms. Further, the experiments show improved receptibility, OOD generalization and less computation overhead as compared to diffuser. Relevant ablations are performed.

Strengths:
Discretizing planning into sub-goals with diffusion models is shown to be advantageous as more diverse scenarios can be solved. Low-level diffusion planning becomes task-agnostic.

Even the sparse diffuser or SD version is better than diffuser because of increased receptive field. The authors validate this by showing that increasing the kernel-size (hence the receptive field) of diffuser leads to better performance but weaker generalization.

SD with dense actions leads to better fitting of the sparse objective, a typical bottleneck in hierarchical RL.

HD takes lesser time than Diffuser due to shorter high-level sequence and parallelly solving low-level plans for all the segments.

Weaknesses:
The formulation has limited novelty. While a single diffuser is not sufficient for planning over long-horizons, the work introduces two diffusers: one sparse diffuser for planning sequence of sub-goals between initial state and goal, while a standard diffuser solves for individual sub-goal segments.

Given that there are also methods which perform state-only diffusion like Decision-diffuser [2], is it possible to perform relevant ablations to justify why states are not sufficient for having a good estimate of $J$? If you are following diffuser’s codebase, are you using the diffusion sampled actions or a separate controller for Maze2d tasks like diffuser?

Solving for sub-goals independently without the knowledge about the final goal might lead to non-optimal behavior. How do you ensure that the sampled path for one segment does not overlap with the paths for the other segment? How many trajectories do you sample for individual segments and for the sub-goal sequence?

Also, what happens when actions do not lead to the exact sub-goal (unless you use inverse dynamics actions) for the maze case? Because there is no-feedback from the low-level diffuser to high-level diffuser, it might become challenging.

Overall, I acknowledge that the presented method is a promising revision of Diffuser which performs better in all aspects. However, I believe that the contributions are not significant enough.

Questions:
See weakness above.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

The algorithm involves using a high-level planner that plans trajectories in a sparser manner and a low-level planner that adds detailed states to the trajectories produces by the high-level planner. Experiment result demonstrate that hierarchical planning produces better performance in long-horizon planning tasks and offline RL tasks. reviews.strengths:
1. The paper is well-written and easy to follow.
2. The method is straightforward and demonstrates strong results in long-horizon planning tasks, especially the AntMaze.
3. The study of relationship between receptive field of the diffusion model and the data coverage ratio is interesting and offers valuable insight for futher research on diffusion models for planning in more complex domains.
reviews.weaknesses:
One minor weakness is that the baseline Decision Diffuser[1] is missing. Also the study presented in this paper focuses on using the CNN architecture as Diffuser does. A comparison between using CNN and transformer architecutre, such as Decision Diffuser, would be encouraged.

[1] Ajay, Anurag, et al. "Is conditional generative modeling all you need for decision-making?." ICLR 2023.
reviews.questions:

Is it the case that the standard Diffuser is able to \textbf{cover} the full data distribution but just fails to achieve full data coverage during actual execution? If so, what might be the cause the such gap?
2. Still concerning Fig 3., although HD seems to cover most of the data distribution, HD fails to cover two short segments/branches. Why does such pheomena occur?

reviews.text:
## Hybrid Review  

**Summary:**  
The paper proposes Hierarchical Diffuser (HD), a hierarchical variant of Diffuser that employs a high-level planner generating sparse "jumpy" subgoals at fixed intervals (K steps) and a low-level planner filling in dense trajectories between subgoals. Experiments demonstrate superior performance in long-horizon planning and offline RL tasks (e.g., +12% on Maze2D, +9.2% on MuJoCo, and +35% on FrankaKitchen versus Diffuser), alongside a 10× planning speedup. The authors also analyze the impact of kernel size in Diffuser and jumpy steps, revealing that small receptive fields fail to cover the full data distribution. Notably, HD exhibits enhanced generalization on compositional out-of-distribution (OOD) tasks, supported by theoretical analysis of generalization-performance tradeoffs.  

**Strengths:**  
1. **Clarity and Presentation:** The paper is well-written, logically structured, and supported by clear visualizations (e.g., Fig. 3–4) and pseudocode.  
2. **Strong Empirical Results:** HD achieves state-of-the-art performance across D4RL benchmarks (e.g., AntMaze, MuJoCo), with significant gains in long-horizon tasks. The OOD generalization experiments (Table 5) are particularly compelling.  
3. **Computational Efficiency:** The hierarchical design enables 10× faster planning by decoupling long-horizon reasoning from local refinement.  
4. **Insightful Analysis:** The study of kernel size and data coverage provides valuable insights into diffusion models’ limitations, while ablations (e.g., dense actions in HD-DA) rigorously validate design choices.  

**Weaknesses:**  
1. **Incomplete Baselines:** The baseline comparison omits Decision Diffuser [1], which uses a transformer architecture. A comparison to this or skill-based hierarchical methods (e.g., latent skill planning) would strengthen the contribution.  
2. **Fixed Subgoal Intervals:** The reliance on fixed K (not adaptive) may limit adaptability in tasks with variable temporal abstractions. While ablations explore K-values (e.g., K=4 for MuJoCo vs. K=15 for Maze2D), heuristics for selecting K systematically are underexplored.  
3. **Dataset Dependency:** Performance is sensitive to dataset quality, and error compounding in hierarchical rollouts remains unaddressed.  
4. **Architectural Scope:** The study focuses solely on CNN-based diffusion models. Contrasting CNN and transformer architectures (e.g., Decision Diffuser) would provide broader insights.  

**Questions:**  
1. **Coverage-Capability Gap (Fig. 3):** Diffuser with small kernels covers one mode during execution but not the full distribution. Is this a coverage failure or an execution gap? If the latter, what causes this discrepancy? HD also misses two short trajectory branches—is this due to fixed subgoals or receptive field constraints?  
2. **Adaptability and Scalability:** How would adaptive K (e.g., via uncertainty estimation) impact performance in tasks with non-uniform temporal abstractions? Could fixed intervals exacerbate compounding errors?  
3. **Theoretical-Practical Alignment:** Theorem 1 links hierarchy to improved generalization. Can this bound be empirically validated (e.g., via suboptimality metrics)? Does HD’s OOD success stem purely from hierarchy or also from receptive field effects?  
4. **Computational Trade-offs:** The low-level planner trains multiple segment diffusers. Does this incur overhead versus Diffuser? For HD-DA, discarding actions after high-level planning seems inefficient—have alternatives (e.g., latent embeddings) been explored?  

**Decision:**  
The paper presents a simple yet effective fusion of hierarchical planning and diffusion models, delivering significant efficiency and generalization gains. While the fixed subgoal interval and architectural limitations warrant discussion, the rigorous benchmarks, speedups, and OOD robustness constitute a solid contribution. However, the omission of key baselines (e.g., Decision Diffuser) and limited exploration of adaptive methods marginally constrain its impact.  

**Rating:**  
**6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold**  

---  
[1] Ajay, Anurag, et al. "Is conditional generative modeling all you need for decision-making?." ICLR 2023.

reviews.summary: The authors of this paper present Hierarchical Diffuser, a simple yet effective hierarchical planning approach using diffusion models to improve the performance and efficiency of diffusion-based planning for long-horizon tasks. By utilizing a two-pronged structure, the Hierarchical Diffuser combines a high-level planner, the Sparse Diffuser, to generate sub-goals, and a low-level planner to refine the plan. This approach significantly outperforms non-hierarchical planning methods, while also enhancing computational efficiency in training and planning speed. Furthermore, the method displays improved generalization capabilities on out-of-distribution tasks compared to non-hierarchical Diffusers.
reviews.strengths:
- Originality: This work creatively combines the strengths of hierarchical planning and diffusion models, achieving significant improvements in performance and computational efficiency compared to existing methods.
- Quality: The contribution provides a thorough theoretical analysis of the method, backed by well-designed experiments and in-depth analyses. The authors emphasize the rationale behind the design decisions and the resultant benefits of their approach.
- Clarity: The paper is well-organized, clearly written, and explains the method along with its advantages and limitations in a coherent and accessible way.
reviews.weaknesses:
- Considering the existence of the HDMI algorithm, the contribution of this paper are limited. However, compared to HDMI, this paper has conducted extensive experiments on generalization and provided corresponding theoretical support. One suggestion I have is that the authors can emphasize the advantages of Hierarchical Diffuser from the perspective of generalization, and highlight the algorithm's generalization performance in the experimental design.
- In the hierarchical framework, the quality of the generated high-level goals is crucial to the effectiveness of the algorithm. The authors emphasize that the Hierarchical Diffuser is simpler compared to existing hierarchical methods but have only designed one goal selection method. In addition to the fixed time interval heuristic method used in this paper, HDMI also mentions two heuristic methods based on spatial intervals and reward scale, which are equally simple and have low implementation costs. If the authors could take these heuristic methods into consideration, I believe it would have a positive impact on the completeness of the paper's content. Furthermore, the demonstration of the quality of the generated goals is essential, and there is a gap in this aspect in the current paper.
reviews.questions:
- I have noticed that both Diffuser and Decision Diffuser employ “inpainting” techniques for trajectory generation in goal-conditioned tasks and the lower-level planner of HDMI. However, this paper utilizes return-guided sampling. What were the considerations behind the authors' choice between the two methods?
- This paper proposes to get the sub-goals first with the planning-based subgoal extractor and then train HDMI with the sub-goals as supervision. Although this two-step process makes sense, the overall training process becomes ad hoc and less general. Is there any connection between the upper and lower layers of Hierarchical Diffuser's planner? Can it be trained end-to-end?
